items.prompt_md: In the transpose-then-reverse rotation, what does the transpose step alone accomplish?
options: It rotates the matrix by 90 degrees directly
It swaps matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i], flipping the matrix across its main diagonal
It reverses the order of the rows
It sorts each row into ascending order
items.answer: It swaps matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i], flipping the matrix across its main diagonal
items.explanation_md: Transpose alone only mirrors the matrix across the main diagonal. A second pass — reversing each row — is what turns that mirrored matrix into a 90-degree clockwise rotation.

items.prompt_md: You must rotate a square matrix 90 degrees clockwise using no extra grid — only O(1) additional space. Which approach fits?
options: Allocate a new matrix and copy each rotated position across
Transpose the matrix in place, then reverse each row
Sort the matrix's values and place them back in row-major order
Use a hash map to remember each cell's original value
items.answer: Transpose the matrix in place, then reverse each row
items.explanation_md: Transpose and row-reversal are both in-place swaps on the existing grid, achieving the O(1)-extra-space rotation the constraint demands. A new matrix or a hash map both cost O(n^2) extra memory.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the in-place transpose-then-reverse rotation for an n x n matrix?
items.explanation_md: Every cell is touched once during transpose and once during row reversal, so time is O(n^2); both passes swap values within the original matrix, so extra space is O(1).
